title: Part-part-whole ratios
description: Use a ratio of parts and a total to find the quantities of individual parts.
topic_path: Get ready for rates & proportional relationships

assessment_items.question_content:
At the zoo, for every $6$ adult camels, there are $5$ baby camels. There are a total of $44$ adult and baby camels at the zoo.

**Complete the table.**

| Original ratio | Number in the zoo
-|-|-
Baby camels | $5$ | [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
Adult camels | $6$ | [[☃ numeric-input 3]]
Total camels | [[☃ numeric-input 1]] | $44$
hints:
Let's find the total number of camels in the original ratio first. 

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

There are $\purpleE5$ baby camels for every $\blueE{6}$ adult camels.

$\purpleE5+\blueE{6}=\maroonE{11} $

The ratio is made up $\maroonE{11}$ total camels.

Let's make an equivalent ratio.

If we have $\redE4$ times as many $\maroonE{\text{total camels}}$ in the whole, we also need $\redE4$ times as many $\purpleE{\text{baby camels}}$.

Also, we need $\redE4$ times as many $\blueE{\text{adult camels}}$.

| Original ratio | | Number in the zoo
-|:-:|:-:|:-:
Baby camels | $\purpleE{5}$ | $\stackrel{\redE{\times 4}}{\rightarrow}$ | $?$
Adult camels | $\blueE{6}$ | $\stackrel{\redE{\times 4}}{\rightarrow}$ | $?$
Total camels | $\maroonE{11}$ | $\stackrel{\redE{\times 4}}{\rightarrow}$ | $44$

This is the completed table.

| Original ratio | Number in the zoo
-|:-:|:-:
Baby camels | $\purpleE{5}$ | $20$
Adult camels | $\blueE{6}$ | $24$
Total camels | $\maroonE{11}$ | $44$

assessment_items.question_content:
Ren has a bag of trail mix made of $54$ total pieces of nut or dried fruit. There are $11$ nuts for every $7$ fruits.

**Complete the table.**

| Original ratio | Number in the bag
-|-|-
Nut pieces | $11$ | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
Fruit pieces | $7$ | [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
Total pieces | [[☃ numeric-input 3]] | $54$
hints:
Let's find the total number of pieces in the original ratio first. 

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

There are $\purpleE{11}$ nut pieces for every $\blueE{7}$ fruit pieces.

$\purpleE{11}+\blueE{7}=\maroonE{18} $

The ratio is made up $\maroonE{18}$ total pieces.

Let's make an equivalent ratio.

If we have $\redE{3}$ times as many $\maroonE{\text{total pieces}}$ in the whole, we also need $\redE{3}$ times as many $\purpleE{\text{nut pieces}}$.

Also, we need $\redE{3}$ times as many $\blueE{\text{fruit pieces}}$.

| Original ratio || Number in the bag
-|-|-
Nut pieces | $\purpleE{11}$ | $\stackrel{\redE{\times 3}}{\rightarrow}$ | $\purpleE{?}$
Fruit pieces | $\blueE{7}$ | $\stackrel{\redE{\times 3}}{\rightarrow}$ | $\blueE{?}$
Total pieces | $\maroonE{18}$ | $\stackrel{\redE{\times 3}}{\rightarrow}$ | $\maroonE{54}$

This is the completed table.

| Original ratio | Number in the bag
-|-|-
Nut pieces | $\purpleE{11}$ | $\purpleE{33}$
Fruit pieces | $\blueE{7}$ | $\blueE{21}$
Total pieces | $\maroonE{18}$ | $\maroonE{54}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Tlaloc pumped up $56$ balls. He pumped up $5$ dodge balls for every $9$ other balls.

**Complete the table.**

| Original ratio | Number Tlaloc pumped up
-|-|-
Dodge balls | $5$ | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
Other balls | $9$ | [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
Total balls | [[☃ numeric-input 3]] | $56$
hints:
Let's find the total number of balls in the original ratio first. 

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

Tlaloc pumped up $\purpleE{5}$ dodge balls for every $\blueE{9}$ other balls.

$\purpleE{5}+\blueE{9}=\maroonE{14} $

The ratio is made up $\maroonE{14}$ total balls.

Let's make an equivalent ratio.

If we have $\redE{4}$ times as many $\maroonE{\text{total balls}}$ in the whole, we also need $\redE{4}$ times as many $\purpleE{\text{dodge balls}}$.

Also, we need $\redE{4}$ times as many $\blueE{\text{other balls}}$.

| Original ratio || Number Tlaloc pumped up
-|-|-
Dodge balls | $\purpleE{5}$ | $\stackrel{\redE{\times 4}}{\rightarrow}$ | $\purpleE{?}$
Other balls | $\blueE{9}$ | $\stackrel{\redE{\times 4}}{\rightarrow}$ | $\blueE{?}$
Total balls | $\maroonE{14}$ | $\stackrel{\redE{\times 4}}{\rightarrow}$ | $\maroonE{56}$

This is the completed table.

| Original ratio | Number Tlaloc pumped up
-|-|-
Dodge balls | $\purpleE{5}$ | $\purpleE{20}$
Other balls | $\blueE{9}$ | $\blueE{36}$
Total balls | $\maroonE{14}$ | $\maroonE{56}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Colton has watched $2$ comedy movies for every $3$ other movies. He has watched $30$ total movies.

**Complete the table.**

| Original ratio | Number Colton has watched
-|-|-
Comedy movies | $2$ | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
Other movies | $3$ | [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
Total movies | [[☃ numeric-input 3]] | $30$
hints:
Let's find the total number of movies in the original ratio first. 

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

Colton has watched $\purpleE{2}$ comedy movies for every $\blueE{3}$ other movies.

$\purpleE{2}+\blueE{3}=\maroonE{5} $

The ratio is made up $\maroonE{5}$ total movies.

Let's make an equivalent ratio.

If we have $\redE{6}$ times as many $\maroonE{\text{total movies}}$ in the whole, we also need $\redE{6}$ times as many $\purpleE{\text{comedy movies}}$.

Also, we need $\redE{6}$ times as many $\blueE{\text{other movies}}$.

| Original ratio | |Number Colton has watched
-|-|-
Comedy movies | $\purpleE{2}$ | $\stackrel{\redE{\times 6}}{\rightarrow}$ | $\purpleE{?}$
Other movies | $\blueE{3}$ | $\stackrel{\redE{\times 6}}{\rightarrow}$ | $\blueE{?}$
Total movies | $\maroonE{5}$ | $\stackrel{\redE{\times 6}}{\rightarrow}$ | $\maroonE{30}$

This is the completed table.

| Original ratio | Number Colton has watched
-|-|-
Comedy movies | $\purpleE{2}$ | $\purpleE{12}$
Other movies | $\blueE{3}$ | $\blueE{18}$
Total movies | $\maroonE{5}$ | $\maroonE{30}$

assessment_items.question_content:
In Sakura's garden, for every $5$ red flowers, there are $10$ yellow flowers. There are a total of $75$ yellow and red flowers in her garden. 

**How many red flowers are in Sakura's garden?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] red flowers
hints:
Let's find the total number of flowers in the original ratio first.

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

There are $\purpleE5$ red flowers for every $\blueE{10}$ yellow flowers.

$\purpleE5+\blueE{10}=\maroonE{15} $

The ratio is made up $\maroonE{15}$ total flowers.

The $\purpleE{\text{part}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{whole}}$ ratio of red flowers to total flowers is $\purpleE5:\maroonE{15}$.

We are looking for an equivalent ratio that has a total of $\maroonE{75}$ flowers.

Let's make an equivalent ratio.

If we have $\redE5$ times as many $\maroonE{\text{total flowers in the whole}}$, we also need $\redE5$ times as many $\purpleE{\text{red flowers in the part}}$.

Red flowers | Total flowers
:-: | :-:
$\purpleE{5}$ | $\maroonE{15}$
$\downarrow \redE{\times 5}$ | $\downarrow \redE{\times 5}$
$?$ | $75$
There are $25$ red flowers in Sakura's garden.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a certain card game, for every $4$ blue cards, there are $3$ yellow cards. There are a total of $84$ blue and yellow cards in the game. 

**How many blue cards are in the game?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] blue cards
hints:
Let's find the total number of cards in the original ratio first.

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

There are $\purpleE4$ blue cards for every $\blueE3$ yellow cards.

$\purpleE4+\blueE3=\maroonE{7} $

The ratio is made up $\maroonE{7}$ total cards.

The $\purpleE{\text{part}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{whole}}$ ratio of blue cards to total cards is $\purpleE4:\maroonE{7}$.

We are looking for an equivalent ratio that has a total of $\maroonE{84}$ cards.

Let's make an equivalent ratio.

If we have $\redE{12}$ times as many $\maroonE{\text{total cards  in the whole}}$, we also need $\redE{12}$ times as many $\purpleE{\text{blue cards in the part}}$.

Blue cards | Total cards
:-: | :-:
$\purpleE{4}$ | $\maroonE{7}$
$\downarrow \redE{\times 12}$ | $\downarrow \redE{\times 12}$
$?$ | $84$
There are $48$ blue cards in the game.

assessment_items.question_content:
At the newest animated movie, for every $9$ children, there are $4$ adults. There are a total of $39$ children and adults at the movie. 

**How many children are at the movie?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] children
hints:
Let's find the total number of people in the original ratio first.

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

There are $\purpleE9$ children for every $\blueE4$ adults

$\purpleE9+\blueE4=\maroonE{13} $

The ratio is made up $\maroonE{13}$ total people.

The $\purpleE{\text{part}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{whole}}$ ratio of children to total people is $\purpleE9:\maroonE{13}$.

We are looking for an equivalent ratio that has a total of $\maroonE{39}$ people.

Let's make an equivalent ratio.

If we have $\redE3$ times as many $\maroonE{\text{total people in the whole}}$, we also need $\redE3$ times as many $\purpleE{\text{children in the part}}$.

Children | Total people
:-: | :-:
$\purpleE{9}$ | $\maroonE{13}$
$\downarrow \redE{\times 3}$ | $\downarrow \redE{\times 3}$
$?$ | $39$
There are $27$ children at the movie.

assessment_items.question_content:
Of a squirrel's hidden nuts, for every $5$ that get found, there are $3$ that do not get found. A squirrel hid $40$ nuts all together.

**How many of the nuts do not get found?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] nuts
hints:
Let's find the total number of nuts in the original ratio first.

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

There are $\purpleE3$ nuts that don't get found for every $\blueE5$ nuts that get found.

$\blueE5+\purpleE3=\maroonE{8} $

The ratio is made up $\maroonE{8}$ total nuts.

The $\purpleE{\text{part}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{whole}}$ ratio of nuts that do not get found to total nuts is $\purpleE3:\maroonE{8}$.

We are looking for an equivalent ratio that has a total of $\maroonE{40}$ nuts.

Let's make an equivalent ratio.

If we have $\redE5$ times as many $\maroonE{\text{total nuts in the whole}}$, we also need $\redE5$ times as many $\purpleE{\text{unfound nuts in the part}}$.

Unfound nuts | Total nuts
:-: | :-:
$\purpleE{3}$ | $\maroonE{8}$
$\downarrow \redE{\times 5}$ | $\downarrow \redE{\times 5}$
$?$ | $40$
There are $15$ nuts that don't get found.

assessment_items.question_content:
At a pond there were  $24$ ducks swimming. The tape diagram shows the ratio of ducklings to adult ducks.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the table.**

| Original ratio | Number in the pond
-|-|-
Ducklings | $5$ | [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
Adult ducks | $1$ | [[☃ numeric-input 3]]
Total ducks | [[☃ numeric-input 4]] | $24$
hints:
Let's find the total number of ducks in the original ratio first. 

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

There are $\purpleE5$ ducklings for every $\blueE1$ adult.

$\purpleE5+\blueE1=\maroonE{6} $

The ratio is made up $\maroonE{6}$ total ducks.

The $\purpleE{\text{part}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{whole}}$ ratio of ducklings to total is $\purpleE5:\maroonE{6}$.

We are looking for an equivalent ratio that has a total of $\maroonE{24}$ ducks.

Let's make an equivalent ratio.

If we have $\redE4$ times as many $\maroonE{\text{total ducks in the whole}}$, we also need $\redE4$ times as many $\purpleE{\text{ducklings in the part}}$.

Also, we need $\blueE4$ times as many $\blueE{\text{adult ducks}}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

|||
-|-|-|-
Ducklings | $\purpleE{5}$ | $\stackrel{\times \redE{4}}\rightarrow$ | $?$
Adult ducks | $\blueE{1}$ | $\stackrel{\times \redE{4}}\rightarrow$ | $?$
Total ducks | $\maroonE{6}$ | $\stackrel{\times \redE{4}}\rightarrow$  | $24$

This is the completed table.

||
-|-|-|-
Ducklings | $\purpleE{5}$ | $20$
Adult ducks | $\blueE{1}$ | $4$
Total ducks | $\maroonE{6}$ | $24$

options.alt: A tape diagram with 2 tapes of unequal lengths. The first tape has 5 equal parts. A curved bracket above the first tape is labeled Ducklings. The second tape has 1 part of the same size as in the first tape. A curved bracket below the second tape is labeled Adult ducks.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tara makes donut topping by mixing sugar and $12\,\text{g}$ of cinnamon. The tape diagram shows the ratio of sugar to cinnamon.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the table.**

| Original ratio | Amount ($\text{g}$)
-|-|-
Sugar | $3$ | [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
Cinnamon | [[☃ numeric-input 3]] | $12$
Total topping | $5$ | [[☃ numeric-input 4]]
hints:
Let's find the parts of the ingredients in the original ratio first. 

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

###Identify the given ratio

There are $\purpleE{3}$ parts of sugar for every $\blueE{2}$ parts of cinnamon.

$\purpleE3+\blueE2=\maroonE{5} $

The ratio is made up $\maroonE{5}$ total parts.

The $\blueE{\text{part}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{whole}}$ ratio of cinnamon to total is $\blueE{2}:\maroonE{5}$.

We are looking for an equivalent ratio that has $\blueE{12\,\text{g}}$ of cinnamon.

Let's make an equivalent ratio.

If we have $\redE{6}$ times as much $\blueE{\text{cinnamon}}$, we also need $\redE{6}$ times as much $\purpleE{\text{sugar}}$.

Also, we will have $\redE{6}$ times as much $\maroonE{\text{total donut topping}}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

| Original ratio | |Amount ($\text{g}$)
-|-|-|-
Sugar | $3$ | $\stackrel{\times \redE{6}}\rightarrow$  | $?$
Cinnamon | $2$ | $\stackrel{\times \redE{6}}\rightarrow$  | $12$
Total topping | $5$ |$\stackrel{\times \redE{6}}\rightarrow$  |  $?$

This is the completed table.

| Original ratio | Amount ($\text{g}$)
-|-|-
Sugar | $3$ | $18$
Cinnamon | $2$ | $12$
Total topping | $5$ | $30$

options.alt: A tape diagram with 2 tapes of unequal lengths. The first tape has 3 equal parts. A curved bracket above the first tape is labeled Sugar. The second tape has 2 equal parts of the same size as in the first tape. A curved bracket below the second tape is labeled Cinnamon.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kali had a total of $21$ solid and striped balloons at her birthday party. The tape diagram shows the ratio of solid balloons to striped balloons.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the table.**

| Original ratio | Balloons at the party
-|-|-
Solid balloons | $4$  | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
Striped balloons | $3$ | [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
Total balloons | [[☃ numeric-input 3]] | $21$
hints:
Let's find the total number of balloons in the original ratio first. 

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

There are $\purpleE4$ solid balloons for every $\blueE{3}$ striped balloons.

$\purpleE4+\blueE{3}=\maroonE{7} $

The ratio is made up $\maroonE{7}$ total balloons.

The $\purpleE{\text{part}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{whole}}$ ratio of solid balloons to total is $\purpleE4:\maroonE{7}$.

We are looking for an equivalent ratio that has a total of $\maroonE{21}$ balloons.

Let's make an equivalent ratio.

If we have $\redE3$ times as many $\maroonE{\text{total balloons in the whole}}$, we also need $\redE3$ times as many $\purpleE{\text{solid balloons in the part}}$.

We also need $\redE{3}$ times as many $\blueE{\text{striped balloons}}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

| Original ratio | | Balloons at the party
-|-|-|-
Solid balloons | $\purpleE{4}$ | $\stackrel{\times \redE{3}}\rightarrow$ | $\purpleE{?}$
Striped balloons | $\blueE{3}$ | $\stackrel{\times \redE{3}}\rightarrow$ | $\blueE{?}$
Total balloons | $\maroonE{7}$ | $\stackrel{\times \redE{3}}\rightarrow$  | $\maroonE{21}$

This is the completed table.

| Original ratio | Balloons at the party
-|-|-|-
Solid balloons | $\purpleE{4}$ | $\purpleE{12}$
Striped balloons | $\blueE{3}$ | $\blueE{9}$
Total balloons | $\maroonE{7}$ | $\maroonE{21}$

options.alt: A tape diagram with 2 tapes of unequal lengths. The first tape has 4 equal parts. A curved bracket above the first tape is labeled Solid balloons. The second tape has 3 equal parts of the same size as in the first tape. A curved bracket below the second tape is labeled Striped balloons.

assessment_items.question_content:
Iara makes salad dressing by mixing $8$ spoonfuls of oil and some vinegar. The tape diagram shows the ratio of oil to vinegar.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the table.**

| Original ratio | Amount (spoonfuls)
-|-|-
Oil  | [[☃ numeric-input 2]] | $8$
Vinegar | $1$ | [[☃ numeric-input 3]]
Total dressing | $3$ | [[☃ numeric-input 4]]
hints:
Let's find the parts of the ingredients in the original ratio first. 

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

###Identify the given ratio

There are $\purpleE{2}$ parts of oil for every $\blueE{1}$ part of vinegar.

$\purpleE{2}+\blueE{1}=\maroonE{3} $

The ratio is made up $\maroonE{3}$ total parts.

The $\purpleE{\text{part}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{whole}}$ ratio of oil to total is $\purpleE{2}:\maroonE{3}$.

We are looking for an equivalent ratio that has $\purpleE{8}$ spoonfuls of oil.

Let's make an equivalent ratio.

If we have $\redE{4}$ times as much $\purpleE{\text{oil}}$, we also need $\redE{4}$ times as much $\blueE{\text{vinegar}}$.

Also, we will have $\redE{4}$ times as much $\maroonE{\text{total dressing}}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

| Original ratio | | Amount (spoonfuls)
-|-|-|-
Oil | $2$ | $\stackrel{\times \redE{4}}\rightarrow$  | $8$
Vinegar | $1$ | $\stackrel{\times \redE{4}}\rightarrow$  | $?$
Total salad dressing | $3$ |$\stackrel{\times \redE{4}}\rightarrow$  |  $?$

This is the completed table.

| Original ratio | Amount (spoonfuls)
-|-|-|-
Oil | $2$ | $8$
Vinegar | $1$ | $4$
Total salad dressing | $3$ | $12$

options.alt: A tape diagram with 2 tapes of unequal lengths. The first tape has 2 equal parts. A curved bracket above the first tape is labeled Oil. The second tape has 1 part of the same size as in the first tape. A curved bracket below the second tape is labeled Vinegar.

assessment_items.question_content:
At a party there are $40$ prizes, which are either kazoos or whistles. The tape diagram shows the ratio of kazoos to whistles.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the table.**

| Original ratio | Prizes at the party
-|-|-
Kazoos | $3$  | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
Whistles | $5$ | [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
Total prizes | [[☃ numeric-input 3]] | $40$
hints:
Let's find the total number of prizes in the original ratio first. 

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

There are $\purpleE{3}$ kazoos for every $\blueE{5}$ whistles.

$\purpleE{3}+\blueE{5}=\maroonE{8} $

The ratio is made up $\maroonE{8}$ total prizes.

The $\purpleE{\text{part}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{whole}}$ ratio of kazoos to total prizes is $\purpleE{3}:\maroonE{8}$.

We are looking for an equivalent ratio that has a total of $\maroonE{40}$ prizes.

Let's make an equivalent ratio.

If we have $\redE{5}$ times as many $\maroonE{\text{total prizes in the whole}}$, we also need $\redE{5}$ times as many $\purpleE{\text{kazoos in the part}}$.

We also need $\redE{5}$ times as many $\blueE{\text{whistles}}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

| Original ratio | | Prizes at the party
-|-|-
Kazoos | $\purpleE{3}$ | $\stackrel{\times \redE{5}}\rightarrow$ | $\purpleE{?}$
Whistles | $\blueE{5}$ | $\stackrel{\times \redE{5}}\rightarrow$ | $\blueE{?}$
Total prizes | $\maroonE{8}$ | $\stackrel{\times \redE{5}}\rightarrow$  | $\maroonE{40}$

This is the completed table.

| Original ratio | Prizes at the party
-|-|-
Kazoos | $\purpleE{3}$ | $\purpleE{15}$
Whistles | $\blueE{5}$ | $\blueE{25}$
Total prizes | $\maroonE{8}$ | $\maroonE{40}$

options.alt: A tape diagram with 2 tapes of unequal lengths. The first tape has 3 equal parts.  A curved bracket above the first tape is labeled Kazoos. The second tape has 5 equal parts of the same size as in the first tape.  A curved bracket below the second tape is labeled Whistles.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table shows the type of super power that $36$ students wish they had. Each student could only pick one super power.

Super power| Students
- | :-:
Flight| $8$
Super strength| $10$
X-ray vision| $6$
Invisibility| $12$

**Match the following ratios to what they describe.** 

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

Let's find the numbers of students in the original ratios first. 

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

###Students who wish they had x-ray vision to all students

The ratio of students who wish they had $\purpleE{\text{x-ray vision}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{all}}$ students is $\purpleE6:\maroonE{36}$.

We can create an equivalent ratio by dividing both parts by $\redE{\text{6}}$.

|||
-|-|-|-
X-ray vision students | $\purpleE{6}$ | $\stackrel{\redE{\div 6}}\rightarrow$ | $\purpleE{1}$
All students | $\maroonE{36}$ | $\stackrel{\redE{\div 6}}\rightarrow$  | $\maroonE{6}$

The ratio of students who wish they had x-ray vision to all students is $1:6$.

###Students who wish for flight to all students

The ratio of students who wish for $\redE{\text{flight}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{all}}$ students is $\redE{8}:\maroonE{36}$.

We can create an equivalent ratio by dividing both parts by $\purpleD{\text{4}}$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Flight students | $\redE{8}$ | $\stackrel{\purpleD{\div 4}}\rightarrow$ | $\redE{2}$
All students | $\maroonE{36}$ | $\stackrel{\purpleD{\div 4}}\rightarrow$  | $\maroonE{9}$

The ratio of students who wish for flight to all students is $2:9$.

###Students who wish for flight to those who wish for invisibility

The ratio of students who wish for $\redE{\text{flight}}$ to students who wish for $\tealE{\text{invisibility}}$ is $\redE{8}:\tealE{12}$.

We can create an equivalent ratio by dividing both parts by $\purpleD{\text{4}}$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Flight students | $\redE{8}$ | $\stackrel{\purpleD{\div 4}}\rightarrow$ | $\redE{2}$
Invisibility students | $\tealE{12}$ | $\stackrel{\purpleD{\div 4}}\rightarrow$  | $\tealE{3}$

The ratio of students who wish for flight to those who wish for invisibility is $2:3$.

###Completed table

Description | Ratio
- | -
The ratio of students who wish they had x-ray vision to all students | $1:6$
The ratio of students who wish for flight to all students | $2:9$
The ratio of students who wish for flight to those who wish for invisibility | $2:3$

left: The ratio of students who wish they had x-ray vision to all students
The ratio of students who wish for flight to all students
The ratio of students who wish for flight to those who wish for invisibility

assessment_items.question_content:
Bobby saw $40$ insects at a picnic and recorded their types in the following table.

Insect| Number 
- | :-:
Fly| $8$
Ant| $22$
Bee | $4$
Ladybug | $6$

**Match the following ratios to what they describe.** 

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

Let's find the numbers of insects in the original ratios first. 

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

###Flies to all insects

The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{flies}}$ to $\blueE{\text{all insects}}$ is $\purpleE8:\blueE{40}$.

We can create an equivalent ratio by $\maroonE{\text{dividing by 8}}$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Flies | $\purpleE{8}$ | $\stackrel{\maroonE{\div 8}}\rightarrow$ | $\purpleE{1}$
All insects | $\blueE{40}$ | $\stackrel{\maroonE{\div 8}}\rightarrow$  | $\blueE{5}$

The ratio of flies to all insects is $1:5$.

###Ladybugs to ants

The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{ladybugs}}$ to $\blueE{\text{ants}}$ is $\purpleE{6}:\blueE{22}$.

We can create an equivalent ratio by $\maroonE{\text{dividing by 2}}$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Ladybugs | $\purpleE{6}$ | $\stackrel{\maroonE{\div 2}}\rightarrow$ | $\purpleE{3}$
Ants | $\blueE{22}$ | $\stackrel{\maroonE{\div 2}}\rightarrow$  | $\blueE{11}$

The ratio of ladybugs to ants is $3:11$.

###Bees to all insects

The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{bees}}$ to $\blueE{\text{all insects}}$ is $\purpleE{4}:\blueE{40}$.

We can create an equivalent ratio by $\maroonE{\text{dividing by 4}}$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Bees | $\purpleE{4}$ | $\stackrel{\maroonE{\div 4}}\rightarrow$ | $\purpleE{1}$
All insects | $\blueE{40}$ | $\stackrel{\maroonE{\div 4}}\rightarrow$  | $\blueE{10}$

The ratio of bees to all insects is $1:10$.

###Completed table

Description | Ratio
- | -
The ratio of flies to all insects | $1:5$
The ratio of ladybugs to ants | $3:11$
The ratio of bees to all insects | $1:10$

left: The ratio of flies to all insects
The ratio of ladybugs to ants
The ratio of bees to all insects

assessment_items.question_content:
The table shows the number of students who signed up for different after school activities. Each student signed up for exactly one activity.

Activity| Students
- | :-:
Cooking| $9$
Chess| $4$
Photography| $8$
Robotics| $11$
**Total** | $32$

**Match the following ratios to what they describe.** 

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

Let's find the numbers of students in the original ratios first. 

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

###Photography students to chess students

The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{photography students}}$ to $\blueE{\text{chess students}}$ is $\purpleE8:\blueE{4}$.

We can create an equivalent ratio by $\maroonE{\text{dividing by 4}}$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Photography students | $\purpleE{8}$ | $\stackrel{\maroonE{\div 4}}\rightarrow$ | $\purpleE{2}$
Chess students | $\blueE{4}$ | $\stackrel{\maroonE{\div 4}}\rightarrow$  | $\blueE{1}$

The ratio of photography students to chess students is $\purpleE{2}:\blueE{1}$.

###Photography students to all students

The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{photography students}}$ to $\blueE{\text{all students}}$ is $\purpleE8:\blueE{32}$.

We can create an equivalent ratio by $\maroonE{\text{dividing by 8}}$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Photography students | $\purpleE{8}$ | $\stackrel{\maroonE{\div 8}}\rightarrow$ | $\purpleE{1}$
All students | $\blueE{32}$ | $\stackrel{\maroonE{\div 8}}\rightarrow$  | $\blueE{4}$

The ratio of photography students to all students is $\purpleE{1}:\blueE{4}$.

###Chess students to all students

The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{chess students}}$ to $\blueE{\text{all students}}$ is $\purpleE4:\blueE{32}$.

We can create an equivalent ratio by $\maroonE{\text{dividing by 4}}$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Chess students | $\purpleE{4}$ | $\stackrel{\maroonE{\div 4}}\rightarrow$ | $\purpleE{1}$
All students | $\blueE{32}$ | $\stackrel{\maroonE{\div 4}}\rightarrow$  | $\blueE{8}$

The ratio of chess students to all students is $\purpleE1:\blueE{8}$.

###Completed table

Description | Ratio
- | -
The ratio of photography students to chess students | $2:1$
The ratio of photography students to all students | $1:4$
The ratio of chess students to all students | $1:8$

left: The ratio of photography students to chess students
The ratio of photography students to all students
The ratio of chess students to all students

assessment_items.question_content:
Quincy was stuck inside for an hour during a storm. He spent $20$ minutes reading,  $18$ minutes eating lunch, $12$ minutes watching television, and $10$ minutes drawing.

**Match the following ratios to what they describe.** 

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

Let's find the numbers of minutes in the original ratios first. 

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

###Time inside to time eating lunch

Quincy was inside for an hour, which is $\purpleE{60}$ minutes.

The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{time inside}}$ to $\redE{\text{time eating lunch}}$ is $\purpleE{60}:\redE{18}$.

We can create an equivalent ratio by $\maroonE{\text{dividing by 6}}$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Total time inside | $\purpleE{60}$ | $\stackrel{\div \maroonE{6}}\rightarrow$  | $\purpleE{10}$
Time eating lunch | $\redE{18}$ | $\stackrel{\div \maroonE{6}}\rightarrow$ | $\redE{3}$

The ratio of time inside to time eating lunch is $\purpleE{10}:\redE{3}$.

###Time eating lunch to time watching television

The ratio of $\redE{\text{time eating lunch}}$ to $\purpleE{\text{time watching television}}$ is $\redE{18}:\purpleE{12}$.

We can create an equivalent ratio by $\maroonE{\text{dividing by 6}}$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Time eating lunch | $\redE{18}$ | $\stackrel{\div \maroonE{6}}\rightarrow$ | $\redE{3}$
Time watching television | $\purpleE{12}$ | $\stackrel{\div \maroonE{6}}\rightarrow$  | $\purpleE{2}$

The ratio of time eating lunch to time watching television is $\redE{3}:\purpleE{2}$.

###Time reading to time drawing

The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{time reading}}$ to $\blueE{\text{time drawing}}$ is $\purpleE{20}:\blueE{10}$.

We can create an equivalent ratio by $\maroonE{\text{dividing by 10}}$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Time reading | $\purpleE{20}$ | $\stackrel{\div \maroonE{10}}\rightarrow$  | $\purpleE{2}$
Time drawing | $\blueE{10}$ | $\stackrel{\div \maroonE{10}}\rightarrow$ | $\blueE{1}$

The ratio of time reading to time drawing is $\purpleE{2}:\blueE{1}$.

###Completed table

Description | Ratio
- | -
The ratio of time inside to time eating lunch | $10:3$
The ratio of time eating lunch to time watching television | $3:2$
The ratio of time reading to time drawing | $2:1$

left: The ratio of time inside to time eating lunch
The ratio of time eating lunch to time watching television
The ratio of time reading to time drawing

assessment_items.question_content:
There were $24$ teams at a soccer tournament. In all, $12$ teams wore red shirts,  $6$ teams wore blue shirts, $4$ teams wore orange shirts, and $2$ teams wore white shirts.

**Match the following ratios to what they describe.** 

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

Let's find the numbers of teams in the original ratios first. 

Then we can find the value to multiply or divide by to create the equivalent ratio.

###Teams wearing orange to teams wearing blue

The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{teams wearing orange}}$ to $\blueE{\text{teams wearing blue}}$ is $\purpleE{4}:\blueE{6}$.

We can create an equivalent ratio by $\maroonE{\text{dividing by 2}}$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Teams wearing orange | $\purpleE{4}$ | $\stackrel{\div \maroonE{2}}\rightarrow$  | $\purpleE{2}$
Teams wearing blue | $\blueE{6}$ | $\stackrel{\div \maroonE{2}}\rightarrow$ | $\blueE{3}$

The ratio of teams wearing orange to teams wearing blue is $\purpleE{2}:\blueE{3}$.

###Teams wearing red to total teams

The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{teams wearing red}}$ to $\blueE{\text{total teams}}$ is $\purpleE{12}:\blueE{24}$.

We can create an equivalent ratio by $\maroonE{\text{dividing by 12}}$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Teams wearing red | $\purpleE{12}$ | $\stackrel{\div \maroonE{12}}\rightarrow$  | $\purpleE{1}$
Total teams | $\blueE{24}$ | $\stackrel{\div \maroonE{12}}\rightarrow$ | $\blueE{2}$

The ratio of teams wearing red to total teams is $\purpleE{1}:\blueE{2}$.

###Teams wearing white to total teams

The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{teams wearing white}}$ to $\blueE{\text{total teams}}$ is $\purpleE{2}:\blueE{24}$.

We can create an equivalent ratio by $\maroonE{\text{dividing by 2}}$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Teams wearing white | $\purpleE{2}$ | $\stackrel{\div \maroonE{2}}\rightarrow$  | $\purpleE{1}$
Total teams | $\blueE{24}$ | $\stackrel{\div \maroonE{2}}\rightarrow$ | $\blueE{12}$

The ratio of teams wearing white to total teams is $\purpleE{1}:\blueE{12}$.

###Completed table

Description | Ratio
- | -
The ratio of teams wearing orange to teams wearing blue | $2:3$
The ratio of teams wearing red to total teams | $1:2$
The ratio of teams wearing white to total teams | $1:12$

left: The ratio of teams wearing orange to teams wearing blue
The ratio of teams wearing red to total teams
The ratio of teams wearing white to total teams
